Biology and Social Science

Tadpoles

MSI Publications

Center for Quantitative Genome Function: About the Alpha Project

In 2002, we established a Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation (CGEC) and began working towards the ability to predict the quantitative behavior of a model, eukaryotic intracellular signaling pathway over time and in response to defined perturbations. In 2006 we changed the name to the Center for Quantitative Genome Function (CQGF) to better reflect our specific goals.  At the Center, we are seeking to learn how genome encoded parts interact with the environment to produce quantitative functions. In so doing, we are devising innovative methods and tools, defining new paradigms for modeling biological systems and contributing to the training of a cadre of leaders in interdisciplinary genomics research. The flagship activity of the Center is the Alpha Project. The project consists of combined experimental and computational work on a well-studied eukaryotic regulatory system, the G-protein receptor-coupled signal transduction pathway that governs the response of haploid MATa S. cerevisiae to the mating pheromone alpha factor. This pathway is a prototype for similar regulatory networks that govern response to extra-cellular stimuli in higher eukaryotes.

Organization of the Center for Quantitative Genome Function (CQGF)
[ View Larger Image ]

The work of the Center occurs at four sites:  Molecular Sciences Institute (MSI), California Institute of Technology (Caltech), the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, (MIT) and the Pacific Northwest National Lab (PNNL). MSI is the core site for all aspects of the project. MSI President, Roger Brent is the principal investigator and Orna Resnekov is the project manager. Drew Endy's lab (MIT) is contributing both systems modeling and experiments. Shuki Bruck's lab (Caltech) is contributing computational methods and theory. Richard Smith's group at the PNNL is contributing mass spectrophotometry methods and measurements. The Center collaborates with Mark Holl of the Microscale Life Sciences Center, who is constructing microfluidic devices that are used by Center scientists. Research for the Alpha project is overseen by a steering committee composed of the PI, co-investigators at the four sites and the project manager. The NHGRI, an external scientific advisor and an external scientific advisory board comprised of distinguished scientists provide critical feedback on the scientific and heuristic goals of the project.

The Center is funded by a five-year renewable grant from the National Human Genome Research Institute (NHGRI).
